content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for a movie in which main character will die, without happy end

**Score:** 82
**Body:** I'm tired of all the cliched movies where the main characters always end up alive and happy. I'd like a more tragic and realistic situation, where ultimately there's no happy ending and the main character dies (or at least that question remains open). The genre is not important. Something like: (spoilers !) >!Taxi Driver, Drive, Midsommar, Birdman, Atonement, Lilya-4Ever, La Haine.!<

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You're kinda spoiling the movies you listed in your opening request, OP. Maybe hide the movie names behind a spoiler tag?
2. Children of Men.
3. Pan’s Labyrinth
  - The sick part about that one is you know, from the very beginning, what happens. Then you forget. Then del Toro slaps you across the face and yells, "I already TOLD you. Remember?!"
4. Rogue One.
5. The Mist is *almost* what you're looking for.
6. Grave of the Fireflies

Fun fact: the family sitting behind me when i first saw it thought it was a fun kids movie, and brought their 5 year old. They were all arguing in the way to the car.
7. To Live And Die In L.A. (1985)
8. No country for old man
9. The Road. Some find the ending hopeful some do not but overall it will definitely give you the despair you are seeking

## 2. What’s the most mind-blowing movie out right now? 🤯

**Score:** 73
**Body:** I’m in the mood for a movie that completely messes with your head — the kind that makes you sit in silence afterwards thinking, what did I just watch…?

It can be new or old, but I’m especially interested in films that still hit hard in 2025.
Something with insane plot twists, genius concepts, or that beautiful “brain-fried” feeling (in the best way possible).

So — what’s the most mind-blowing movie you’ve seen recently? Drop your best recommendations! 🍿


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arrival
2. It can only happen once, but... The Sixth Sense.  

Once you know, you can never go back.
  - The same as The Game with Michael Douglas and Sean Penn. 
3. Memento
4. Muholland drive. Still don’t know what I watched
  - And to think it’s just training wheels for Inland Empire! (I’m not saying which I think is a better film, just that the level of “what the heck did I just watch” is turned way, way up in Inland Empire)
5. Incendies
6. Oldboy 2003
7. You’re looking for Primer.

Or totally differently, Perfect Blue.
8. Coherence 

## 3. movies about people who got lost in the wild and fought the elements

**Score:** 41
**Body:** apart from “the revenant” and “cast away”, i want movies about people who got lost in the wild and fought the elements, animals, loneliness, etc, thanks in advance 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Edge (1997) stars Anthony Hopkins and Alec Baldwin
  - I still think about the scene where he says he can make fire from ice and then does it
  - Great movie.
2. “Jeremiah Johnson” (1972) starring Robert Redford. Set in the mid-1800s, a man decides to go west, live in the mountains, and brave the elements (and unexpected hostile forces). Slow-paced by modern standards but still very enjoyable.

Bonus: Also the source of the beaded guy slow nod of approval meme.
3. **Wild** (2014) Reese Witherspoon

**Into The Wild** (2007)
4. The Grey (2011)
5. Society of the Snow (2023)
6. Jungle (2017)
7. Not a movie, but the old YA book “Hatchet” is worth reading (and rereading).

I love the survival genre, and have seen most of the movies in this thread. Hatchet surpasses them all for me. 

Easy read, and it won a newberry, the top tier young adult book award. I love how we get to experience the survivor’s thoughts and mental revelations as they are forced to adapt to the wilderness.
8. The Emerald Forest (1985)

Alive (1993)

## 4. Need a dark weird movie

**Score:** 38
**Body:** I’m going to be traveling and want to download a movie for the plane. 

Vibes I’m looking for: I’m obsessed with Tim Burton-esque type movies, loved Pan’s Labyrinth, love shows like Cabinet of Curiosities and Black Mirror. Looking for anything weird and esoteric and macabre. Horror-ish but with a fantasy twist or element. 

Edit: A lot of Donnie Darko suggestions, and I’ve seen it multiple times, love it!! Glad you’re catching the vibes. Thank you all for the amazing recs. Can’t wait to look some of these up. Definitely looking back on this post for future movie nights! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. City of the lost children
2. Dark City
3. Donnie Darko
4. Bubba hotep
5. Brazil.
6. Try the show Twin Peaks
7. The Cell. (2000)

If you haven't seen it, I think this might be your answer.
8. Repo! The Genetic Opera

Crimson Peak 

Franklyn
9. Lynch: Blue Velvet, Lost Highway, Fire Walk With Me, Mulholland Drive.
10. Suspiria

## 5. A really scary movie that's NOT Gross

**Score:** 28
**Body:** 

Hey guys, I'm in the mood for a scary horror movie, one that actually creeps you out. My problem is I hate bodygore/bodyhorror or anything that's too gross. Especially vomit, as I have severe emetophobia. So maybe something that's more psychological or creepy monsters. My faves are when the evil thing are humans. Maybe you guys can help me 😊

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Others (2001)
2. If you haven't watched, John Carpenter's  *In the Mouth of Madness* is a good one without gore. You may also like *The Fog*.

*The Vanishing* (1988, the Dutch original) is also a good one.
  - I'd second The Fog!
3. So I just rewatched The Mothman Prophecies with Richard Gere. A great, underrated Supernatural horror/thriller. Very spooky, at times really scary in a "what the hell is going on" kind of way and zero gore. Wish there were more films like it.
4. The Vanishing (1988), Dutch
5. The Others with Nicole Kidman. It’s eerie as hell, super atmospheric, and totally relies on tension and mystery instead of gore.
6. Blair Witch Project
7. The Haunting (1963 version)
8. It follows, Get out (it’s more thriller like), The Babadook
9. The Devils Playground

The Orphanage

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What's your favorite Yelling quote in films?

**Score:** 1562

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Yes they deserved to die, and I hope they burn in hell.”
2. KHAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAN
3. “First, take a big step back and literally FUCK YOUR OWN FACE!”
4. Sam Rockwell's guttural scream when he goes on-board the alien ship in Galaxy Quest
5. We got no food, we got no jobs, OUR PETS’ HEADS ARE FALLING OFF!!!
6. HEY YOU GUYS!
7. LOUD NOISES!
8. The whole time. The whole time. The whole time?!
9. YOU...ARE...A...TOY!!!!
10. “…it’s an inanimate object…”
“YOU’RE AN INANIMATE FUCKING OBJECT!”

In Bruges

## 2. What actor/actress is known for being comedic, yet is also surprisingly very good at playing more “serious” roles?

**Score:** 429

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. John C. Reilly
  - Gangs of New York and Magnolia off the top of my head. He is incredible in them.
2. Public service announcement that people should watch Barry, because that show is goddamn incredible and in my top 10. Hader blew me away in that role
3. Bryan Cranston
4. Odenkirk
5. I think I'm the first to say Steve Carell. His acting in The Patient was great.
6. Robin Williams
  - One Hour Photo was a pretty sick flick
7. I'll say Jim Carrey since no one has yet.  Also, it's said that comedic acting can be more difficult than dramatic acting.  It's also said that some of the funniest comedians are actually the saddest or deepest, and that's why they can be so funny.  And I think people like Robin Williams or Jim Carrey prove that.
8. Adam Sandler has brought both tears and laughter out of me in equal measure.

## 3. What is the most accurate depiction of a profession in film?

**Score:** 396

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clerks
  - “I hate customers.”
2. My Cousin Vinny 1992
  - This should be the highest answer. Isn't it shown on law degrees as an example of successful cross examination and asking the right questions to discredit somebody's testimony?
3. I was an internal IT Auditor for a big tech company for 5 years.  So OfficeSpace really resonates with me when the Bob’s say “What is it… you do here.”
4. The Big Short is very close to how corporate finance/banking works.
5. Waiting
6. Full Metal Jacket really did a good job of portraying boot camp.
7. The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the 8th Dimension
8. Generation Kill.  

Each time a  character came on screen, I said to myself, "o, hey, I served with a guy like that."

Every time they complained about a situation, I thought, "yeah, that's what the daily gripe sessions sounded like."

## 4. With a score of 15% on Rotten Tomatoes, this movie is proof that a low RT score does not predict how funny a movie is.

**Score:** 348

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'll smoke it with you bro, we'll go to the looney bin together, I don't give a fuck!
  - Im way to baked to drive to the devils house
2. 
The scene when Doris Robert’s is under the bed kills me every time
3. How can he see me?
4. This movie is an absolute classic.
  - Why are you naked?
  - 85% audience score on Rotten Tomatoes.  I don't know why we are even talking about the critic store when the people have spoken. 

I just realized there is a major difference between critics and moviegoers that didn't dawn on me till now. They are trying to watch *as many movies as possible*, while I and probably my most moviegoers are not. We're trying to pick the best of 10 or so in theaters at any given time and are going to pick a genre/title that seems like a safe bet.

Still though, if you can't enjoy grandma's boy and it gets an 85% audience review, then you're just not a good reviewer.
5. Alex, you forgot smoking lamp
6. "It says High Score. Is that a good thing? Did I break it?"
7. I can't stop cumming!

## 5. Who’s a movie or TV character that’s fully aware they’re evil, but believes they are “necessary evil”?

**Score:** 317

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Operative from “Serenity”
  - "That is a trap. I offer money, you'll play the man of honor and take umbrage; I ask you to do what is right and you'll play the brigand. I have no stomach for games."
  - "I'm a monster"
  - Damn. Glad I'm not the only one still thinking about that dude. An almost perfect example.
2. Ozymandias in Watchmen

EDIT: Also, the comedian in watchmen
  - I'd say at the end Dr Manhattan felt the same.
3. Al Pacino's Lucifer:

*"I'm here on the ground with my nose in it since the whole thing began. I've nurtured every sensation man's been inspired to have. I cared about what he wanted and I never judged him. Why? Because I never rejected him. In spite of all his imperfections, I'm a fan of man!"*
  - I missed this movie on its release, but watching this, other than Pacino's Heat, shocked it doesn't get more play. Ironically this goes toe to toe with Stormare's bored and exhausted Lucifer also acting with Keanu Reeves in Constantine.
4. Vader absolutely knows. 

I would argue Tywin did not see himself as evil. He was ruthless and pragmatic. 
5. Thanos believes he is doing what needs to be done.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Frankenstein (2025): A film that robs it’s subject matter of it’s poetic nature

**Score:** 87
**Body:** Up until now, I’ve had great hopes for Frankenstein (2025). I’m quite a Del Toro fan, with *Pan’s Labyrinth* and the *Hellboy* films being particular favourites. Del Toro has always had an affinity for finding the humanity in monsters we would typically consider grotesque, but here, Del Toro allowed his love for the monster to get the better of him, to the point of removing any nuance or contrast within this character. 

As a fan of the original Mary Shelley novel, the contrast between this monstrous being and an incredibly tragic backstory has always been fascinating. The fact that such a grotesque, monstrous creature is an abandoned, vulnerable child lent the novel a horrifically poetic quality. But while Del Toro’s adaptation has received praise for ‘humanising’ The Creature, my experience of watching the film left me with the conclusion that Del Toro went too far in the opposite direction, to the point of robbing the character of the contrasts which have made him such a fascinating character across decades.

The Creature, as played by Jacob Elordi, is barely a monster. He rarely commits any violent actors throughout the film, and in 95% of instances, any violent acts are committed in complete self-defence. Instead of making you question who the real monster is, the film draws its conclusion for you, with characters literally calling Doctor Frankenstein the real monster. But the biggest misstep for me is the design of the Creature. Jacob Elordi is hard to fault in his performance, but instead of allowing his eyes alone convey the vulnerability of the character, The Creature essentially looks like Jacob Elordi in white makeup (with a green hue). The effect this leaves us with, is that whenever Elordi is on the screen, we barely feel like we’re watching a monster, or an experiment gone wrong.

I admire Del Toro’s ambitions here, but in the process of humanising Frankenstein more than any previous adaptation, he has lost something crucial. It leaves you with a lingering question: At what point has the monster simply become too human? And in robbing Frankenstein’s Monster of the crucial poetic tension that exists between his humanity and his grotesque nature, how much of a disservice has he done to the character?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I enjoyed reading that and I'm looking forward to seeing the film. The reviews I've read seem to echo what you're saying.

I wonder if GDT just loves his monsters a bit too much? The monster in Frankenstein (the novel) is a sympathetic character, but he is very much still a monster. He does kill innocent people. GDT's approach to humanising his monsters is in danger of becoming a bit of a schtick. Why can't they be sympathetic AND monstrous, in the way that humans can be? That to me is much more interesting than just a misunderstood ugly guy who only kills in self defense.
  - Well said! Feels like in his attempt to humanise the Creature, he only made him more one-dimensional. Hope to hear your thoughts once you watch the film.
  - This would require a sophistication and nuance in his screenplays that he is not only incapable of (he’s a bad writer), but seems actively disinterested in. Coupled with the over-lit, CGI aesthetics of his recent output, this is what makes his movies (Pan’s Labyrinth and Cronos aside) such shallow experiences.
2. I more or less agree here. I feel similar to Coppola’s Dracula. Where it’s probably my favorite adaptation of the book, and it’s very beautiful, but I loathe the changes/interpretations made to it. I was really hoping for a loyal adaptation of the novel but this is not that. 

To me, I think the whole focus on whether or not it’s a “monster” is missing the point though. I just reread the book and the creature shows a knack for deep introspection and petty violence. Ironically,  sounds pretty human to me. I agree with you that they shouldn’t have strayed away from the creature’s darkness, because through that we actually explore the human condition more. Where we are both capable of great good and great evil. The film seems like it’s trying to take a black and white stance and beats you with “he’s a victim! the doctor is the real monster!” That’s not that interesting to me. 

Overall, very well put. I do recommends everyone sees it though. Definitely not a bad movie. 
  - I agree. In the novel, neither Victor nor the monster are all bad or all good. Victor’s main crime results from youthful exuberance as much as anything, and his punishment is clearly massively disproportionate. It’s true that when conducting experiments on bringing life to dead matter, he probably shouldn’t have STARTED with a 7-foot tall super-strong man made of exhumed corpses. He should have first trialled it on a frog or something. But because he’s young and foolish, we never got Frankenstein’s Frog. He makes a massive mistake, but he’s no more the villain than the monster is the hero.
  - > To me, I think the whole focus on whether or not it’s a “monster” is missing the point though. I just reread the book and the creature shows a knack for deep introspection and petty violence. Ironically,  sounds pretty human to me

Those were pretty much my thoughts when I read the book. I saw the monster as a microcosm of humanity - a thinking creature thrust into a harsh world with no idea of what made him or if he has a purpose, capable of both great tenderness and artistry and the senseless violence and cruelty of the rest of the animal kingdom
3. It did also lend a certain absurdity to every instance the monster was attacked without provocation.

"What... is that?"

"I think... a reasonably tall man in a cloak? If I squint, I think he has some facial scarring?"

"Unload on it!"
  - This is unfortunately true.
4. Well put! I'll add that robbing the creature of its monstrous nature weakens Victor's inner torment and makes him a less interesting character by extension. Instead of facing the "What monstrosity have I created?" problem, Victor is left with a less compelling and more convoluted, >!"What monstrous deeds have I done in order to create this really cool thing that fails to meet my expectations...which, actually, in the end, turns out to perfectly meet my expectations except that it's way more likeable than I am, dammit, and maybe even hotter?"!<

On a psychological level, I heard someone muse that Del Toro's leading man creatures are self-inserts, which makes sense why he'd remove Frankenstein's Creature's more horrifying qualities.
5. I'm torn on this film. I had very high hopes going in since I consider Del Toro's Pinocchio a masterpiece, or very close to it. I assumed if he could do that with Pinocchio, he would have no problems adapting Frankenstein into an amazing modern visual story. During the first ~25 minutes of the film I was loving every moment of it. As the story unfolded, I became less enthralled. I agree with your point that the creature is barely a monster, I remember sitting there thinking "why did they make Frankenstein hot?" 

Overall I still very much enjoyed the film and would probably give it a solid 4/5, but IMO it was not the 5/5 I was hoping for. That being said, it's definitely worth a watch despite it's tiny flaws. The cinematography was amazing and the pacing never felt too slow or too fast.

## 2. Women in Kiyoshi Kurosawa’s Cure

**Score:** 15
**Body:** The film begins with a prostitute’s murder and a detective saying, “it was bound to happen.” 

The next murder is of another woman, this time by her husband. The next, a woman is the one hypnotized to murdered. Mamiya calls on her memory of her dissection of a man during her medical school training, and how it was her first time seeing a man naked. He brings up how she was held back from becoming a surgeon because of misogyny. 

Takabe, the protagonist, has a mentally ill wife, who he eventually proclaims out loud that she is a burden to him. 

When he’s in the laundromat there’s a focus on a red dress. I’m not sure how to interpret this, any thoughts? 

All of this to say, what is the role of women in this film? If the film is about the frustration and evil inside of us, I wonder if it also has to do with the pervasive misogyny in society. Why did there need to be a line about how the prostitute was bound to be murdered? Because if men see women as something to buy, then is the next logical step something they can use to satisfy their darker urges? I find it interesting that later there are only men murdering one another. Another inevitability of the patriarchy? 

I’m not sure if Kurosawa was touching on these themes intentionally but after hearing that opening line after the prostitutes murder, it echoed for me throughout the film. What do you think?   

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was so enamored with the movie during my watch I failed to make the observations you did, so regrettably I don’t have much to add but I agree with your initial assessments.  

After watching Cloud recently, I’m quite confident Kurosawa is intentional with his text and subtext (although this one felt pretty blunt) having specific meaning. 
  - I just watched Pulse and decided to rewatch Cure, and only on this watch I had these observations. I guess that’s the beauty of art that it becomes something different for every person who watches :). I definitely have to watch the rest of his filmography because just from those two films I can see his artistic voice… so good!!
2. A little correction: the third murder in the movie is the policeman at the post who shoots his coworker.

The thing with all of those murders is to show that there is always a reason to them, no matter how petty. It is topic of one the conversationss between Sakuma and Takabe: why do people do that? Who knows, sometimes the culprits don't even know themselves. 

However, as the movie progresses. Each the events leading to each subsequent murder are shown in more detail to us and we learn that everybody has reasons behind their bursts of violence, even if they aren't fully aware of them or they may seem irrational.

On a first watch, it is not obvious, but on a rewatch, the teacher shows clear signa of being the jealous type, going by how he rreacts to Mamiya noting his wife's looks with a nervous 'you were looking at her?'. The next two murders are obvious in their motivation: the policeman hated how his buddy wouldn't let him do things like smoke while on duty, as seen during Mamiya's stay and the doctor wishes she could have been a surgeon.  Takabe reveals that he hates how his wife has made his off work life am exhausting labor. The most mysterious murders, the first an last one, are easy to guess: the waitress at the end presumably was fed up with the pressure of her daily work (waiting tables is a stressful job) and going by the comment by the policeman (sex workers are indeed constantly vulnerable to violence from their customers) and how he is found hiding naked, it is very likely the salaryman was afraid to get caught by his family/social circle and killed the sex worker on an initial impulse to try get rid of the  cause of his distress.

Remember, it is a movie set in late 20th century Japan, where outward politeness and emotional hermetism are the expected norm. The movie posits that it is not surprising tbat such a social environment is goong to be ineffectual at discovering the underlying causes behind passional crimes. We see another example of this repressed discomtwnt at the dry cleaner with the customer waiting right before Takabe who mutters all sorts of discontwnt with the employee and the peceived tardiness of the service only to then put a smile before saying thank you and leaving the store.

I think Takabe could have very well have murdered Sakuma and the cop so as to get ahold of Mamiya's method. Then again, and I think this has already been pointed out,  Sakuma and Mamiya can be seen as representations of the need for an official/reasonable explanation and the selfish need to get rid of emotional distress/disregard for societal rules (Mamiya also represents Takabe's need for an explanation in the absence of an official one), respectively, with the ending showing Takabe moving on from his workaholic cop who needs a reason for everything (Sakuma's death) before ultimately deciding that 'well, shit happens' and stop caring as much, prioritizing his own enjoyment instead. Thus him murdering the now also useless Mamiya and ending the movie going on about his life, visibly free of any previous distress and literally going on about his life while a murder is about to take place next to him. More selfish, probably also less good at his job, but happier.
  - Thanks for your reply and the correction! 

This movie is definitely one you should rewatch, maybe multiple times because I still missed some of the subtext that you’ve pointed out! Since you seem to have a good grasp on what the film is saying, what do you think about that shot in the laundromat later on with the red dress? I don’t think that the themes of misogyny/sexual violence were all that pervasive so that’s why I wanted to clarify I’m not sure if this was what he intended with the film at all, but the red dress feels like such a specific image to focus on, even for a moment. 

Since it is about society, I think I could have a fair point that misogyny is another part of the evil that lives within us, and it comes out with “the cure” of the hypnosis, even if it’s not exactly what Kurosawa was going for. The woman who failed to become a surgeon’s reasons were definitely because of self doubt as a woman in a society that favours men. She’s able to unleash her anger about this through her hypnosis.

Super interesting film!!
3. I think you're spot on in your conclusions and the other comments do a really good job expanding on the subtext and how the hypnotic suggestions Mamiya is doing speaks to each characters subconscious. 


I'll also take the opportunity of someone posting about Cure to link my favorite video essay - https://vimeo.com/238019139

---
